Representing the United States Olympic Games 2008 Beijing Team 2012 London Team 2024 Paris Team 2004 Athens Team FIBA World Championship 2006 Japan FIBA Americas Championship 2007 Las Vegas LeBron Raymone James ( / l ə ˈ b r ɒ n / [ 1 ] lə- BRON ; born December 30, 1984) is an American professional basketball player for the Los Angeles Lakers of the National Basketball Association (NBA). Nicknamed " King James ", he is the NBA's all-time leading scorer and has won four NBA championships from 10 NBA Finals appearances, including eight consecutive appearances between 2011 and 2018. [ 2 ] He has also won three Olympic gold medals as a member of the U.S. national team . James is widely considered to be one of the greatest basketball players of all time. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] In addition to ranking fourth in NBA career assists and sixth in NBA career steals , James holds several individual honors, including four NBA MVP awards, four Finals MVP awards, the Rookie of the Year award, three All-Star Game MVP awards, the inaugural NBA Cup MVP, and the Olympics MVP in the 2024 Summer Olympics . A record 22-time All-Star and 21-time All-NBA selection (including a record 13 First Team selections), he has also made six All-Defensive Teams . The oldest active player in the NBA , he holds records for the most seasons played with 23, the most games played , the most minutes played , and the most field goals made in league history. Born and raised in Akron, Ohio , James gained national attention at St. Vincent–St. Mary High School and was heavily touted as a future NBA superstar for his all-around scoring, passing, athleticism and playmaking abilities. [ 5 ] A prep-to-pro , the Cleveland Cavaliers selected James with the first overall pick of the 2003 NBA draft . He won Rookie of the Year and quickly established himself as one of the league's premier players, leading Cleveland to its first NBA Finals appearance in 2007 and winning the scoring title in 2008. After winning back-to-back MVPs in 2009 and 2010, he joined the Miami Heat as a free agent in 2010, a controversial move announced in the television special The Decision . [ 6 ] With the Heat, James won his first two NBA championships in 2012 and 2013 , earning MVP and Finals MVP honors both years. After four seasons in Miami, he returned to Cleveland in 2014, leading the Cavaliers to their first-ever championship in 2016 , ending the Cleveland sports curse . He signed with the Lakers in 2018, winning another title in 2020 and becoming the first player to win Finals MVP with three different teams. Off the court, James has earned further wealth and fame from numerous endorsement contracts. He is the first player in NBA history to accumulate $1 billion in earnings as an active player. [ 7 ] He was among Time ' s 100 most influential people in the world in 2005, 2013, 2017, and 2019—the most selections for a professional athlete. Early life LeBron Raymone James was born on December 30, 1984, [ a ] at Cleveland Clinic Akron General [ b ] in Akron, Ohio , [ 9 ] to 16-year-old Gloria Marie James. [ 10 ] : 22 His father, Anthony McClelland, has an extensive criminal record that includes arson and theft, and was not involved in James' life. [ c ] When James was growing up, life was often a struggle for the family, as they moved from apartment to apartment in the more run-down neighborhoods of Akron while Gloria struggled to find steady work. [ 15 ] Realizing that her son would be better off in a more stable family environment, Gloria allowed him to move in with the family of Frank Walker, a local youth football coach who introduced James to basketball when he was nine years old. [ 10 ] : 23 James began playing organized basketball in the fifth grade. [ 16 ] He later played Amateur Athletic Union (AAU) basketball for the Northeast Ohio Shooting Stars. [ 15 ] The team enjoyed success on a local and national level, led by James and his friends Sian Cotton, Dru Joyce III , and Willie McGee. [ 10 ] : 24 The group dubbed themselves the "Fab Four" and promised each other that they would attend high school together. [ 10 ] : 27 In a move that stirred local controversy, they chose to attend St. Vincent–St. Mary High School , a private Catholic school with predominantly white students. [ 17 ] High school Basketball As a 6-foot-2-inch (1.88 m) tall freshman, James averaged 18 points and 6 rebounds per game for the St. Vincent–St. Mary varsity basketball team. [ 18 ] The team, known as The Fighting Irish, went 27–0 en route to the Division III state title, making them the only boys high school team in Ohio to finish the season undefeated. [ 19 ] As a sophomore, James averaged 25.2 points and 7.2 rebounds, along with 5.8 assists and 3.8 steals per game. [ 18 ] For some home games during the season, St. Vincent–St. Mary played at the University of Akron 's 5,492-seat Rhodes Arena to satisfy ticket demand from alumni and fans, as well as college and NBA scouts who wanted to see James play. [ 20 ] The Fighting Irish finished the season 26–1 and repeated as state champions. [ 18 ] For his outstanding play, James was named Ohio Mr. Basketball and selected to the USA Today All-USA First Team , becoming the first sophomore to achieve either. [ 18 ] In 2001, during the summer before his junior year, James was the subject of a feature article in Slam magazine. Writer Ryan Jones lauded the 16-year-old James, who had grown to 6 feet 7 inches (2.01 m), as "[possibly] the best high school basketball player in America right now". [ 21 ] During the season, James also appeared on the cover of Sports Illustrated , becoming the first high school basketball underclassman to do so. [ 10 ] : 104 With averages of 29 points, 8.3 rebounds, 5.7 assists, and 3.3 steals per game, he was again named Ohio Mr. Basketball and selected to the USA Today All-USA First Team, [ 18 ] and became the first junior to be named male basketball Gatorade National Player of the Year . [ 10 ] : 117 St. Vincent–St. Mary finished the year with a 23–4 record, ending their season with a loss in the Division II championship game. [ 10 ] : 114 Following the loss, James unsuccessfully petitioned for a change to the NBA's draft eligibility rules in an attempt to enter the 2002 NBA draft . [ 22 ] [ 23 ] During this time, he used marijuana , which he said was to help cope with the stress that resulted from the constant media attention he was receiving. [ 24 ] During his senior year, James and the Fighting Irish played against several nationally ranked teams, including Oak Hill Academy on December 12, 2002. The game was nationally televised on ESPN2 . [ 10 ] : 142 Time Warner Cable , looking to capitalize on James' popularity, offered St. Vincent–St. Mary's games to Ohio-based subscribers for $7.95 per game on a pay-per-view basis throughout the season, but this ended up not being profitable. [ 10 ] : 143 [ 25 ] For the year, James averaged 31.6 points, 9.6 rebounds, 4.6 assists, and 3.4 steals per game, [ 18 ] was named Ohio Mr. Basketball and selected to the USA Today All-USA First Team for an unprecedented third consecutive year, [ 10 ] : 178 and was named Gatorade National Player of the Year for the second consecutive year. [ 18 ] He participated in three year-end high school basketball all-star games—the EA Sports Roundball Classic , the Jordan Brand Capital Classic , and the McDonald's All-American Game —losing his National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) eligibility and making it official that he would enter the 2003 NBA draft . [ 26 ] James was at the center of several controversies during his senior year. For his 18th birthday, James skirted state amateur bylaws by accepting a Hummer H2 as a gift from his mother, who had secured a loan for the vehicle by utilizing James' future earning power as an NBA player. [ 27 ] This prompted an investigation by the Ohio High School Athletic Association (OHSAA) because its guidelines stated that no amateur may accept any gift valued over $100 as a reward for athletic performance. James was cleared of any wrongdoing because he had accepted the vehicle from a family member and not from an agent or any outside source. [ 26 ] Later in the season, James accepted two throwback jerseys worth $845 from an urban clothing store in exchange for posing for pictures, violating OHSAA rules and resulting in his being stripped of his high school sports eligibility. [ 26 ] James appealed the ruling and his penalty was eventually dropped to a two-game suspension, allowing him to play the remainder of the year. The Irish were also forced to forfeit one of their wins, their only official loss that season. [ 28 ] In his first game back after the suspension, James scored a career-high 52 points. [ 29 ] St. Vincent–St. Mary won the Division II championship, marking their third division title in four years. [ 30 ] Football As an underclassman, James played wide receiver for St. Vincent–St. Mary's football team. [ 10 ] : 51 He was recruited by some Division I programs , including Notre Dame . [ 31 ] At the end of his second year, James was named first team all-state, and as a junior, he helped lead the Fighting Irish to the state semifinals. [ 32 ] James did not play during his senior year because of a wrist injury that he sustained in an AAU basketball game. [ 33 ] Some sports analysts, football critics, high school coaches, as well as former and current professional players have speculated that James could have played in the National Football League (NFL). [ d ] Professional career Cleveland Cavaliers (2003–2010) 2003–2004: Rookie of the Year The Cleveland Cavaliers selected James as the first overall pick of the 2003 NBA draft . [ 38 ] James chose jersey number 23 in honor of Michael Jordan . [ 39 ] In his first regular season game, James scored 25 points in a 106–92 loss to the Sacramento Kings , setting an NBA record for the most points scored by a prep-to-pro player in his debut performance. [ 40 ] At the conclusion of the 2003–04 season , James became the first Cavalier to receive the NBA Rookie of the Year Award . [ 41 ] and only the third player in league history to average at least 20 points, five rebounds, and five assists per game as a rookie. [ 42 ] 2004–2008: Rise to superstardom In the 2004–05 season , James earned his first NBA All-Star Game selection, contributing 13 points, 8 rebounds, and 6 assists in a winning effort for the Eastern Conference . [ 43 ] Teams took note of his rapid development, and Denver Nuggets coach George Karl told Sports Illustrated : "It's weird talking about a 20-year-old kid being a great player, but he is a great player ... He's the exception to almost every rule." [ 44 ] On March 20, James scored 56 points against the Toronto Raptors , setting Cleveland's new single-game points record. [ 45 ] At the end of the season, James was named to his first All-NBA Team . [ 32 ] [ 45 ] The Cavaliers again failed to make the playoffs, finishing the season 42–40. [ 46 ] During the 2006 offseason, James signed a three-year, $60 million contract extension with the Cavaliers, with the option for a fourth year. [ 47 ] At the 2006 All-Star Game , James led the Eastern Conference to victory with 29 points and was named the NBA All-Star Game Most Valuable Player (MVP). [ 48 ] He finished second in overall NBA MVP Award voting, behind Steve Nash . [ 49 ] Under James' leadership, the Cavaliers qualified for the playoffs for the first time since 1998. [ 50 ] In his postseason debut, James recorded a triple-double in a win over the Washington Wizards . [ 51 ] In Game 3, he made the first game-winning basket of his career, and another in Game 5. [ 52 ] Cleveland defeated the Wizards before losing to the Detroit Pistons in the second round. [ 53 ] James engages in a pre-game ritual of tossing crushed chalk into the air in March 2008. [ 54 ] [ 55 ] The Cavaliers finished the 2006–07 season with 50 wins and entered the playoffs as the second seed in the Eastern Conference. [ 56 ] In the first two rounds, James led the team to wins over the Wizards and New Jersey Nets , earning them a matchup with the Pistons in the Eastern Conference finals. In Game 5, James logged 48 points with nine rebounds and seven assists in a 109–107 double-overtime win. He scored the last 25 points for the Cavaliers, and performed a game-winning layup with 2.2 seconds left. [ 57 ] In 2012, ESPN ranked James' performance as the fourth greatest in modern NBA playoff history. [ 58 ] The Cavaliers won the series in six games, granting them their first NBA Finals appearance, which pitted them against the San Antonio Spurs . [ 59 ] [ 60 ] The Spurs defeated the Cavaliers in a four-game sweep . [ 61 ] On December 11, 2007, James came off the bench for the first time in his career. [ e ] He chose to enter the game at the same time as his teammate Anderson Varejão —who had been involved in a pay dispute with Cavaliers management—to prevent fans from booing Varejão. [ 62 ] In February of the 2007–08 season , James was named All-Star Game MVP for the second time after a 27-point, eight-rebound, and nine-assist performance. [ 63 ] On March 21, he surpassed Brad Daugherty as the Cavaliers' all-time leading scorer. [ 64 ] James' 30 points per game were the highest in the league, marking his first scoring title. [ 65 ] [ 66 ] Seeded fourth in the East entering the playoffs , the Cavaliers defeated the Wizards, before being eliminated by the Boston Celtics in the next round. [ 67 ] [ 68 ] 2008–2010: MVP seasons At the end of the 2008–2009 season , James finished second in the voting for the NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award . He made his first NBA All-Defensive Team , with 23 chase-down blocks and a career-high 93 total blocks. [ 69 ] [ 70 ] [ 71 ] With James' play and the acquisition of All-Star guard Mo Williams , Cleveland went a franchise record 66–16; James became the first Cavalier to win the MVP Award. [ 72 ] [ 73 ] John Hollinger of ESPN described James' season as "arguably the greatest individual season in history". [ 74 ] In the 2009 playoffs , Cleveland swept the Pistons and the Atlanta Hawks to earn a matchup with the Orlando Magic in the Eastern Conference Finals. [ 75 ] The Cavaliers lost Game 1, despite James scoring 49 points on 66 percent shooting. [ 58 ] In Game 2, James hit a game-winner to tie the series 1–1. [ 76 ] Cleveland lost the series in six games; following the loss, James left the court without shaking hands with his opponents, which was viewed as unsportsmanlike by some. [ 77 ] [ 78 ] [ 79 ] James averaged 35.3 points per game during the playoffs. [ 80 ] In February of the 2009–10 season , James was forced into a temporary point guard role following injuries to players in the Cavaliers' backcourt. [ 81 ] With James' leadership, Cleveland finished the year with the best record in the league. [ 82 ] Despite only playing 39 minutes per game, James received his second NBA MVP Award. [ 83 ] To open the playoffs , Cleveland advanced past the Chicago Bulls to earn a matchup with the Celtics in the second round. [ 84 ] James was criticized for not playing well in Game 5, shooting only 20 percent on 14 shots and scoring 15 points. [ 85 ] The Cavaliers suffered their worst loss in franchise history; at the conclusion, James walked off the court to boos from Cleveland's crowd. [ 86 ] The Cavaliers were eliminated from the postseason in Game 6. [ 84 ] Miami Heat (2010–2014) The Decision Main article: The Decision (TV program) James with the Cavaliers in 2009. [ 87 ] James became an unrestricted free agent on July 1, 2010. [ 88 ] He was contacted by several teams, including the Bulls, Los Angeles Clippers , Miami Heat , New York Knicks , New Jersey Nets , and Cavaliers. [ 89 ] On July 8, James announced on a live ESPN special titled The Decision that he would sign with the Heat. [ 90 ] The special was broadcast from the Boys & Girls Club of Greenwich, Connecticut and raised $2.5 million for the organization. An additional $3.5 million was raised from advertising revenue, and was donated to other charities. [ 91 ] The day before the special aired, fellow free agents Chris Bosh and Dwyane Wade announced they would sign with Miami. [ 92 ] [ 93 ] James joined with Bosh and Wade in part so he could shoulder less of the offensive load, and he thought he would have a better chance of winning an NBA championship with the Heat. [ 94 ] Heat president Pat Riley played a major role in convincing James to play on the team with Bosh and Wade. [ 95 ] James would be relieved of the burden of scoring, and thought he could be the first player since Oscar Robertson to average a triple-double in a season. [ 94 ] James, Bosh and Wade were referred to as an NBA superteam . [ 96 ] Upon leaving the Cavaliers, James drew criticism from executives, fans, and current and former players. [ 97 ] The Decision was viewed as unnecessary. Many thought the prolonged wait for James' choice was unprofessional, as not even the teams courting him were aware of his decision until moments before the show. [ 98 ] Upon learning that James would not be returning to Cleveland, Cavaliers owner Dan Gilbert published a letter to fans in which he denounced James' actions. [ 99 ] Some angry fans of the team recorded videos of themselves burning his jersey. [ 100 ] Former NBA players, including Michael Jordan and Magic Johnson , were critical of James, condemning him for forming a superteam with Bosh and Wade in Miami and not trying to win a championship in Cleveland. [ 101 ] [ 102 ] [ 103 ] Some commentators defended James' decision to join Miami by noting the Cavaliers' inability to build strong support around him. They argued other NBA greats had benefitted from talented teammates. [ 104 ] James drew further criticism in an interview with CNN when he claimed race might have been a factor in the fallout from The Decision . [ 105 ] [ 106 ] The phrase "taking my talents to South Beach" became a punch line for critics. [ 107 ] [ 108 ] James later expressed regret over his handling of The Decision . [ f ] 2010–2011: Year of media and fan scrutiny James goes in for a slam dunk as a member of the Heat in March 2011. James signed a 6-year, $110 million contract with the Heat on July 10, 2010. [ g ] He changed his jersey number to 6, as the number 23 was retired in Miami for Michael Jordan . [ 116 ] [ 117 ] The Heat threw a welcome party for the " Big Three " at the American Airlines Arena , during which James declared that the Heat would win multiple championships. [ h ] Outside Miami, the spectacle was badly received, furthering negative perception of James. [ 121 ] Throughout the 2010–11 season , the media and opposing fans treated James as a villain. [ 122 ] The Heat struggled to adjust to these new circumstances, going only 9–8 after 17 games. [ 123 ] James later admitted the negativity made him play with an angrier demeanor. [ 122 ] On December 2, James faced the Cavaliers in Cleveland for the first time since departing. [ 124 ] He scored 38 points and led Miami to victory while being booed every time he touched the ball. [ 125 ] The Heat turned their season around and finished as the East's second seed. [ 126 ] In the Eastern Conference Semifinals , the Heat was matched with the Boston Celtics . [ 127 ] In Game 5, James scored Miami's last 10 points to seal a series-clinching win. [ 128 ] After the buzzer, James knelt in an emotional moment, later telling reporters it was an extremely personal victory for him and the team. [ 129 ] The Heat advanced to the Finals , where they lost to the Dallas Mavericks . [ 130 ] James' Finals average of 17.8 points per game was a 9-point drop from the regular season, the largest drop-off in league history. [ 131 ] [ 132 ] 2011–2013: Back-to-back championships The 2011–12 season was delayed by a lockout . During that summer, James worked with Hakeem Olajuwon to improve his post up game. [ i ] As the start of the season approached, James set a goal for himself to leave behind the "villain role" that he had embraced, and said he was hoping to regain a sense of joy on the court. [ 122 ] Miami began the year with a franchise-best 18–6 record, and James was named MVP for the third time. [ j ] In the second round of the playoffs , Miami lost Bosh to an abdominal injury and found themselves trailing the Indiana Pacers two games to one. [ 137 ] James responded with a 40-point, 18-rebound, and nine-assist outing in Game 4 to even the series. [ 138 ] To compensate for Bosh's absence, the Heat embraced a small-ball lineup with James at power forward , which they retained after Bosh's return in the conference finals against the Celtics . [ 139 ] [ 140 ] Facing elimination in Game 6, James recorded 45 points and 15 rebounds to lead the Heat to victory in a "career-defining performance". [ 141 ] Miami won Game 7 to advance to the Finals , earning them a matchup with the Oklahoma City Thunder . [ 142 ] [ 143 ] In Game 5, James registered a triple-double as the Heat defeated the Thunder for their second-ever championship and James' first. [ 144 ] James was voted Finals MVP with averages of 28.6 points, 10.2 rebounds, and 7.4 assists. [ 145 ] His full postseason run was ranked the second best in modern NBA history. [ 146 ] During January of the 2012–13 season , James became the youngest player in NBA history to score 20,000 career points. [ 147 ] In February, he averaged 29.7 points and 7.8 assists while setting shooting efficiency records. [ 148 ] [ 149 ] The Heat began a 27-game winning streak, which is the third longest in NBA history , and James' performance was described as a "month for the ages". [ 150 ] [ 151 ] Miami finished with a franchise and league best 66–16 record, [ 152 ] and James was named MVP for the fourth time, falling one vote shy of becoming the first player to win unanimously. [ 153 ] The Heat won the Eastern Conference Finals against the Pacers, then faced the Spurs in the Finals . [ 154 ] James was criticized for his lack of aggressiveness and poor shot selection, as Miami fell behind 2–3. [ k ] In Game 6, James recorded his second triple-double of the series, including 16 fourth quarter points, to lead the Heat to a comeback victory. [ 157 ] The Heat proceeded to win Game 7, marking their second consecutive championship. [ 158 ] James was named Finals MVP for the second straight season. [ 159 ] 2013–2014: Final season in Miami On March 3 of the 2013–14 season , James scored a career-high 61 points against the Charlotte Bobcats . [ 160 ] He also set a record for the most three-point field goals made in a 60-point game. Miami earned their fourth consecutive Finals berth, again facing the Spurs. [ l ] In Game 2, James led the Heat to a series-tying victory with 35 points on a 64 percent shooting rate. [ 165 ] San Antonio defeated the Heat in five games, ending Miami's quest for three consecutive championships. [ 166 ] Return to Cleveland (2014–2018) In June 2014, James opted out of his contract with the Heat and became an unrestricted free agent. [ 167 ] He returned to the Cavaliers, signing a two-year, $42 million contract, and switched back to the No. 23 jersey. [ m ] In contrast to his departure four years earlier, his announcement to return to Cleveland was well received. [ n ] 2014–2016: Ending Cleveland's championship drought James takes a shot in November 2014. During that season, James became the Cavaliers' all-time assists leader. [ 176 ] [ 177 ] In January of the 2014–15 season , James missed two weeks due to knee and back strains, which was the longest stretch of missed games in his career at the time. [ 178 ] The Cavaliers advanced to the Finals against the Golden State Warriors , making James the first player since the 1960s to play in five consecutive Finals. [ 179 ] [ 180 ] Cleveland took a 2–1 series lead, [ 181 ] but lost in six games. [ 182 ] During the 2015 offseason, James declined his $21.5 million player option and re-signed with Cleveland on a two-year, $47 million contract that carried a second year player option. [ 183 ] During the 2015–16 season , there was speculation that James played a role in the firing of Cavaliers coach David Blatt . [ 184 ] [ 185 ] Despite this distraction, Cleveland finished with 57 wins and the best record in the East. [ 186 ] The Cavaliers advanced to the NBA Finals , losing only two games en route to a rematch with the Warriors, who were coming off a record-setting 73-win season. [ 186 ] [ 187 ] To begin the series, Cleveland fell behind 3–1. [ 188 ] James responded by registering 41-point games in Games 5 and 6, leading the Cavaliers to consecutive wins. [ 189 ] In Game 7, he posted a triple-double and made a clutch block on the Warriors' Andre Iguodala as Cleveland claimed victory, becoming the first NBA team to come back from a 3–1 deficit in the Finals. The win also marked the city of Cleveland's first professional sports title in 52 years. [ 190 ] [ 191 ] James became the third player to record a triple-double in an NBA Finals Game 7, and averaged 29.7 points, 11.3 rebounds, 8.9 assists, 2.3 blocks, and 2.6 steals during the series. He was unanimously selected as the Finals MVP. [ 192 ] [ 193 ] During a White House ceremony honoring the Cavaliers for their victory, President Obama praised James' work ethic, determination, selflessness and his "insistence on always making the right play." [ 194 ] 2016–2018: End of second stint in Cleveland James opted out of his player option and re-signed with the Cavaliers on a three-year, $100 million contract, in which the final year was a player option. [ 195 ] The 2016–17 season was marred by injuries and unexpected losses for the Cavaliers; [ 196 ] James described it as one of the strangest years of his career. [ 197 ] [ 198 ] Cleveland finished the season as the East's second seed, with James averaging 26.4 points and career highs in rebounds (8.6), assists (8.7), and turnovers (4.1) per game. [ 80 ] In Game 3 of the first round of the playoffs , James led Cleveland to a comeback victory against the Pacers . [ 199 ] In Game 5 of the Eastern Conference Finals against the Celtics , James scored 35 points and surpassed Michael Jordan as the NBA's all-time postseason scoring leader. The Cavaliers advanced to the NBA Finals for the third consecutive year, again facing the Warriors . [ 200 ] In Game 4, James logged a triple-double of 31 points, 10 rebounds, and 11 assists, as Cleveland staved off elimination. With this performance, James passed Magic Johnson for the most NBA Finals triple-doubles in a career. [ 201 ] The Cavaliers lost the Finals in five games, despite James's double-double in game 5. Player profile Standing 6 feet 9 inches (2.06 m) tall and weighing 250 pounds (113 kg), [ 413 ] James has played the majority of his career at the small forward and power forward positions, [ 80 ] but he has also been deployed at other positions when necessary. [ 414 ] His playing style, which is athletic and versatile, [ 134 ] has drawn comparisons to Basketball Hall of Fame players Oscar Robertson , Magic Johnson and Michael Jordan . [ ai ] Offense As an 18-year-old rookie, James led the Cavaliers in scoring. [ 418 ] He holds numerous "youngest to" distinctions, [ 419 ] [ 420 ] including being the youngest player to score 30,000 career points. [ 421 ] During his first stint in Cleveland, James was primarily used as an on-ball point forward , and although his shooting tendencies were perimeter-oriented, [ 134 ] he established himself as one of the best slashers and finishers in basketball. [ 422 ] [ 423 ] His combination of speed and size often created matchup problems for opposing teams because James was capable of charging past larger defenders and overpowering smaller ones. [ 424 ] James was frequently criticized for not having a reliable jump shot or post game. [ citation needed ] Teams would try to exploit these weaknesses by forcing him to settle for three-pointers and long two-pointers, a strategy famously used by Spurs coach Gregg Popovich in the 2007 Finals, during which James scored on only 36 percent of his two-point field goal attempts, and on only 20 percent of his three-point attempts. [ 425 ] James drives to the basket in March 2008. A deft finisher, he led the NBA in scoring and shooting percentage at the rim in 2013. [ 426 ] In Miami, Heat coach Erik Spoelstra changed James' role to a more unconventional one. [ 134 ] James spent more time in the post and improved his shot selection and accuracy on jump shots. [ 134 ] [ 427 ] He also learned how to work as an off-ball cutter in the Heat's "pass-happy" offense. [ 428 ] With these improvements, James' overall scoring efficiency rose significantly. [ 429 ] During this time, ESPN's Tom Haberstroh called James' free-throw shooting his biggest weakness. [ 430 ] In 2015 and 2016, James began to experience subtle age-related declines in productivity, [ 431 ] posting his lowest scoring averages since his rookie season. [ 80 ] [ 432 ] His shooting also temporarily regressed, and he briefly ranked as the NBA's worst high-volume shooter from outside the key . [ 433 ] Despite these changes, he remained an elite offensive player who beat defenses with body control, strength, and varying attack speeds. [ 424 ] For most of his career, James has controlled the offense as the primary ball handler on his team. [ 434 ] His playmaking ability is generally considered one of his premier skills, and some analysts rank him among the greatest passers in NBA history. [ 435 ] By exploiting his size, vision, and the attention he garners from opposing defenses, [ 436 ] he creates easy points for his teammates with precise assists. [ 437 ] James executes unconventional passes, including after leaving his feet and through defensive traffic. [ 438 ] His uncanny tendency to find an open player has pushed opposing teams to incorporate elements of zone defense into their strategies. [ 424 ] Early in his career, James was criticized for overpassing in pressure situations, in particular for passing instead of shooting in the waning seconds of close games; [ 439 ] [ 440 ] however, as his career progressed, James' clutch performance was viewed more favorably. [ 441 ] Defense James defending against Joe Harris in February 2018 At the beginning of his NBA career, James was considered a poor defensive player, [ 442 ] but he improved steadily over the years. In 2009, he demonstrated proficiency at the chase-down block . [ 70 ] In Miami, he developed into a more versatile defensive player, and the Heat relied on him to guard all five positions. [ 443 ] Between 2012 and 2016, he was ranked among the top three defenders in the NBA by general managers, and was named the best perimeter defender in the league ahead of the 2014–15 season . [ 444 ] Along with Shane Battier and Dwyane Wade, Miami used James in an ultra-aggressive defensive scheme, [ 445 ] with James cheating off the ball to help out inside or to get into rebounding position. [ 446 ] Beginning in 2014, some analysts saw a regression in his defensive impact, stemming from a lack of effort and expected age-related declines. [ 447 ] [ 448 ] During his second stint in Cleveland, James' defense progressively declined. After missed drives on offense, he often dawdled back on defense while complaining to the referees; he provided less help off the ball, and was less aggressive in switching . [ 449 ] James himself admitted to taking plays off at times, referring to this approach as "chill mode". [ 450 ] He eventually developed a reputation for raising his defensive impact in the playoffs, which some analysts referred to as "Playoff LeBron". [ 451 ] Legacy James with the Cavaliers in 2017. According to NBA analyst Brian Windhorst , "No one has ever had as much hype as James has had to live up to, and James has delivered on every last drop." [ 452 ] James left high school as one of the most hyped prospects in NBA history. [ aj ] Upon entering the league, he made an immediate impact and was voted Rookie of the Year in his debut season. [ 457 ] As of 2024, James has been named to 21 All-NBA Teams, including 13 times to the First Team, which are both NBA records. [ 267 ] His four MVP awards are matched only by Michael Jordan, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, Wilt Chamberlain, and Bill Russell ; James and Russell are the only players to win four MVP awards in a five-year span. [ 458 ] James has also won four Finals MVP Awards, which is the second-most all-time, [ 459 ] and earned All-Defensive honors every season from 2009 to 2014. [ 80 ] While James has never won the Defensive Player of the Year Award, he has finished second in the voting twice and lists it among his goals. [ 69 ] [ 460 ] His teams have appeared in the Finals ten times and won four championships; his 10 Finals appearances are tied for third all-time. [ ak ] Some analysts have criticized him for not having a better Finals record, while others have countered that James usually performed well but his team was defeated by superior competition. [ al ] Based on his career longevity and on-court performances, sports publications have consistently included James in rankings of the best basketball players in history, [ am ] and he was named the Associated Press Male Athlete of the Decade for the 2010s. [ 472 ] His high rankings have resulted in frequent debates about whether James or Michael Jordan is the greatest player of all time. [ an ] In a 2016 interview with Sports Illustrated , James said he was hoping to surpass Jordan as the greatest player. [ 473 ] In February 2018, The Ringer spent an entire week devoted to both players, with Bill Simmons ultimately concluding that Jordan was still ahead. [ 474 ] James has ranked second behind Jordan in polls, the results of which show a strong correlation with the age of respondents, as older voters more often choose Jordan. [ ao ] Business Insider stated that the data "would suggest that younger, more-engaged NBA fans lean toward James, as he's still playing" while older generations "who watched Jordan play and tune in less today lean toward Jordan." [ 478 ] [ 477 ] Referring to James as the best challenger to Jordan's status as the greatest player, Sam Quinn of CBS Sports stated that "in the rings-obsessed basketball discourse", Jordan having more titles and an "unblemished Finals record holds significant weight". [ 473 ] In addition to praising James' on-court accomplishments, analysts have also noted his influence on player empowerment throughout the NBA, which stemmed from his willingness to change teams during free agency. Ben Golliver of The Washington Post said that James' move to the Heat in 2010 "defined a decade of player movement", and that he "fundamentally flipped the power balance between stars and their organizations." [ 479 ] Players have also remarked on his influence, such as Warriors forward Draymond Green , who reflected: "We've taken control of our destiny. And I think a lot of people hate that ... I think the doors that he's opened for athletes and especially basketball players is his biggest accomplishment." [ 480 ] Multiple current and former NBA players have suggested James' free agency decisions catalyzed the league-wide trend in " superteams " in the 21st century. [ 558 ] [ 559 ] In 2023, SpringHill and Warner Bros. released a reboot of the House Party film series, which contains a cameo by James. [ 560 ] [ 561 ] James was the executive producer of rapper 2 Chainz 's 2019 album Rap or Go to the League . [ 562 ] A press release said the album celebrates "black excellence" while challenging "the notion that the only way out of the inner city is either to become a rapper or a ball player." [ 563 ] In June 2022, it was announced that James was launching a media company, Hana Kuma, in partnership with the professional tennis player Naomi Osaka and Maverick Carter. [ 564 ] In March 2024, James and JJ Redick launched a podcast called Mind the Game , where the two have "pure conversations about basketball." [ au ] The podcast was suspended after Redick was hired as head coach for the Los Angeles Lakers in June 2024. [ 568 ] In March 2025, a second season was announced with Steve Nash replacing Redick as James' co-host. [ 569 ] Investments and other ventures In 2012, James, Maverick Carter and Paul Wachter made an investment of less than $1 million in the Pasadena -based fast casual chain Blaze Pizza ; their investment had grown to $25 million by 2017. [ 570 ] James later became a spokesman for the company and began appearing in advertisements after ending his contract with McDonald's. [ 570 ] James partnered with Arnold Schwarzenegger in 2018 to found Ladder, a company that developed nutritional supplements to help athletes with severe cramps, which James experienced during the 2014 NBA Finals . [ 571 ] During the 2019 offseason, James filed for a trademark on the term " Taco Tuesday " for use in downloadable audio and visual works, podcasts, social media, online marketing, and entertainment services. [ 572 ] James had used the term on Instagram to refer to his family's taco dinners. The request was denied by the United States Patent and Trademark Office , stating that "Taco Tuesday" was "a commonplace term, message or expression widely used by a variety of sources that merely conveys an ordinary, familiar, well-recognized concept or sentiment." [ 573 ] In November 2020, James became an angel investor of the tequila and mezcal company Lobos 1707 . [ 574 ] After personal frustration with comments on the Black Lives Matter movement made by Republican U.S. senator Kelly Loeffler , who at the time was the owner of the WNBA 's Atlanta Dream , James assisted Dream player Renee Montgomery in her successful bid to buy the team in March 2021. [ 575 ] That same year, James joined Fenway Sports Group as a partner, making him a part-owner of the Boston Red Sox , New England Sports Network , RFK Racing , and Liverpool F.C .; he had already owned a two-percent share in Liverpool F.C. individually. [ 576 ] The investment made James and Carter the company's first black partners. [ 576 ] In June 2022, James said that once he finishes playing basketball, he would like to own an NBA team located in Las Vegas, Nevada , either through expansion or relocation. [ 577 ] However, in March 2026 he said he was not interested in being part of an ownership group for a Las Vegas franchise. [ 578 ] In August 2022, James and the rapper Drake became part owners of the Italian soccer club A.C. Milan . [ 579 ] James is also a part owner of the Pittsburgh Penguins of the National Hockey League . [ 580 ] Professional contracts James' first agent was Aaron Goodwin . After leaving Goodwin in 2005, James formed the sports marketing company LRMR with his childhood friends Maverick Carter, Ernest "Randy" Mims and Rich Paul . LRMR handles James' marketing, which included the much-criticized television special The Decision . [ 581 ] [ 582 ] Following his departure from Goodwin, James had signed with agent Leon Rose , who joined Creative Artists Agency (CAA) in 2007. Rose worked with fellow CAA agent Henry Thomas, who represented Dwyane Wade and Chris Bosh, to bring James to Miami in 2010. In 2012, James left CAA and became a client of Paul and the agency he founded, Klutch Sports Group . [ 583 ] [ 584 ] Throughout his career, James has taken a unique approach to his NBA contracts, usually opting to sign shorter-term deals in order to maximize his earnings potential and flexibility. [ av ] For example, in 2006 James and the Cavaliers negotiated a three-year, $60 million contract extension instead of the four-year maximum as it allotted him the option of seeking a new contract worth more money as an unrestricted free agent following the 2010 season. [ 587 ] This move allowed James, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh to sign together with the Heat. [ 588 ] During the 2011 NBA lockout , James received contract offers to play professional football from the Dallas Cowboys and Seattle Seahawks ; [ 589 ] he gave the offers serious consideration and even began training with them in mind. [ 590 ] During his second stint in Cleveland, based on a negotiation strategy devised by NBA agent Mark Termini, [ 591 ] James began re-signing [ 592 ] [ 593 ] a new contract each season to take advantage of higher salaries resulting from the NBA's rising salary cap. [ 168 ] In 2016, he signed with the Cavaliers on a three-year contract, [ 594 ] which made him the highest-paid player in the league for the first time in his career. [ 595 ] Philanthropy James has maintained close ties to his hometown of Akron, Ohio, which is also where his charity foundation, the LeBron James Family Foundation (LJFF), is based. [ 596 ] Since 2005, the foundation has held an annual bike-a-thon to raise money for various causes. [ 597 ] In 2015, James announced a partnership with the University of Akron to provide scholarships for up to 2,300 children beginning in 2021. [ 598 ] In 2017, he received the J. Walter Kennedy Citizenship Award from the NBA for his "outstanding service and dedication to the community." [ 599 ] In November 2017, the Akron School Board approved the I Promise School , a public elementary school created in a partnership with the LJFF to help struggling students stay in school. [ 600 ] The school opened on July 30, 2018, with James reflecting that it was his most important professional accomplishment. [ 601 ] Other initiatives undertaken by James in Akron include the I Promise Institute at the University of Akron, the I Promise Village housing complex, the House Three-Thirty community center and retail plaza, and the I Promise Health Quarters medical center. [ aw ] James is also an active supporter of various non-profit organizations, including After-School All-Stars , Boys & Girls Clubs of America and Children's Defense Fund . [ ax ] In 2016, he donated $2.5 million to the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture to support an exhibit on Muhammad Ali . [ 609 ] Social activism James has taken public stances on various social issues—many of them controversial—and has mentioned a feeling of obligation to effect change using his status. [ 610 ] These issues include the War in Darfur , [ 611 ] the killing of Trayvon Martin , [ 612 ] Donald Sterling 's racist comments in 2014, [ 610 ] the Michael Brown verdict, [ 613 ] the death of Eric Garner , [ 614 ] the U.S. national anthem kneeling protests , [ 615 ] the shooting of Breonna Taylor , [ 616 ] the death of Ma'Khia Bryant , [ 617 ] Kyle Rittenhouse , [ 618 ] the Houston Astros sign stealing scandal and the 2022 U.S. Supreme Court ruling that overturned Roe v. Wade . [ ay ] After a racial slur was painted on the gate of James' Brentwood home in 2017, he commented on the difficulty of being black in the United States. He said, "We got a long way to go for us as a society and for us as African Americans until we feel equal in America." [ 622 ] [ 623 ] In 2018, Fox News host Laura Ingraham told James to "shut up and dribble" in response to his political statements. [ 624 ] That same year, James used Ingraham's statement as the title for a Showtime documentary film series he produced, Shut Up and Dribble , which examines the role of athletes in politics and culture. [ 625 ] [ 626 ] In October 2019, NBA executive Daryl Morey expressed support for pro-democracy protesters in Hong Kong . After James said Morey was "misinformed", James was criticized by protesters in the movement, with many using racist language against him on social media. [ az ] In February 2022, political commentator Bill Maher called James hypocritical for not taking a critical stance towards the Chinese government's human rights abuses. [ 632 ] On August 27, 2020, James and his Lakers teammates, as well as the Milwaukee Bucks , began boycotting the 2020 NBA playoffs to protest the shooting of Jacob Blake . [ 633 ] In response, senior White House advisor Jared Kushner said he planned to reach out to James regarding the boycott. Following a players' committee to discuss the boycott, James and others spoke with former president Barack Obama , who reportedly advised them to continue playing and finish the NBA season. [ 634 ] In October 2023, following the October 7th attacks in Israel , James voiced his support for Israel on Instagram. [ 635 ] His statement drew backlash for its omission of the Palestinians affected by the ensuing Gaza war , including from the Palestinian-American activist Omar Suleiman . [ ba ] Politics In June 2008, James donated $20,000 to a committee in support of Barack Obama's 2008 U.S. presidential election campaign. [ 639 ] Later that year, James gathered almost 20,000 people at Rocket Arena [ bb ] for a free Jay-Z concert and a viewing of Obama's 30-minute American Stories, American Solutions television advertisement. [ 640 ] Eight years later, James campaigned for Hillary Clinton during the 2016 U.S. presidential election . [ 641 ] In 2017, James called U.S. President Donald Trump a "bum" after Trump withdrew a White House invitation to Stephen Curry . [ 642 ] The following year, James accused Trump of attempting to divide the country with sports, and declared that he would never sit across from Trump. [ bc ] In August 2020, James announced his support for the campaign of Joe Biden for the 2020 U.S. presidential election . [ 646 ] James spoke in support of the More Than a Vote movement, encouraging African-Americans to vote and criticizing voter suppression . [ 647 ] [ 648 ] In October 2024, James announced his support for Kamala Harris in the 2024 U.S. presidential election . [ 649 ] Personal life James married his high school sweetheart, Savannah James ( née Brinson ; born August 27, 1986), on September 14, 2013, in San Diego, California. [ 650 ] They have two sons, Bronny and Bryce , and a daughter, Zhuri. [ 651 ] [ 652 ] Bronny was a standout high school basketball player, and was named as a McDonald's All-American in 2023, 20 years after James' selection. [ 653 ] James owns a 30,000 square foot (2,787 m 2 ) mansion in Akron, Ohio, that he custom-built in 2003 at a cost of $2.1 million. [ 654 ] During his stint with the Heat, James resided in Coconut Grove , where he bought a $9 million three-story mansion overlooking Biscayne Bay . [ 655 ] In November 2015, he bought a 9,350 square-foot (869 m 2 ) East Coast-style mansion in Brentwood, Los Angeles , for $21 million. [ 656 ] He purchased another home in Brentwood in December 2017 for $23 million. [ 657 ] In September 2020, he purchased a third home in Los Angeles, this one in Beverly Hills , for $36.75 million. [ 658 ] James' closest friends in the NBA are Carmelo Anthony, Chris Paul , and his former Heat teammate Dwyane Wade. The four have been referred to as the "banana boat crew" . During an excursion to the Bahamas , James rescued Anthony when he was carried away from their boat by a current. Anthony credited James with saving his life. [ 659 ] In September 2023, new client names from the Biogenesis scandal were released, including that of Ernest "Randy" Mims, a longtime friend and business manager of James, and David Alexander, a well-known trainer of prominent athletes who co-owned a cold-pressed juice and smoothie business with Savannah and also served as her personal trainer. The DEA determined that there "was never any indication that LeBron James did anything wrong." [ 660 ] [ 661 ] According to James' business partner Maverick Carter, James was in the habit of spending $1.5 million per year on his body, as reported by Men's Health in 2021. This figure included paying for personal chefs and trainers as well as recovery therapies such as cryotherapy and hyperbaric chambers. [ 662 ] In a March 2025 interview, James denied that he spends up to $1 million per year on his health. [ 663 ] He told ESPN in 2018 that he drinks wine every night, believing it to be good for his heart . [ 664 ] He also has a fondness for sweets, especially chocolate chip cookies. [ bd ] While experiencing sciatica in late 2025, James removed desserts and alcohol from his diet for two months. [ 665 ] [ 667 ] In June 2009, he underwent surgery at the Cleveland Clinic to remove a benign tumor in the parotid gland on the right side of his jaw.
